home.title: BRIEF
home.tagline: Scientific Decks · Bilingual Content · Conference-ready Output
home.descriptionLead: BRIEF is a bilingual presentation system for scientific talks, conference materials, and structured publication-ready delivery.
home.descriptionBody: It supports creating, presenting, and sharing professional slide flows with simple controls and clear localization.
home.overviewTitle: BRIEF is a presentation and poster hub for academic communication.
home.overviewBody: It brings together a full-screen conference presentation mode, speaker notes, and poster-oriented materials in one static, portable, GitHub Pages-ready interface.
home.presentationCardTitle: A Rights-Based Governance Framework for AI-Enabled Human Enhancement
home.presentationCardBody: A full 15-slide deck focused on governance, rights, risk, and operational constraints for AI-enabled human enhancement.
home.posterCardTitle: Ethical and legal aspects of AIgmented HUMANity
home.posterCardBody: A poster-oriented view for the same research theme, prepared for concise, visual academic presentation.

presentation.title: BRIEF presentation system
presentation.lead: This section is intended to present a single deck, its context, and the essential information for the audience.
presentation.description: You can place a conference presentation, talk summary, abstract, and supporting references here. The layout stays simple, static, and ready for GitHub Pages deployment.
presentation.slideOneTitle: The Slide component keeps a strict 16:9 frame
presentation.slideOneBody: Each slide fills the browser step, while the actual canvas remains constrained to a 16:9 area. On other viewport ratios, the extra space stays outside the slide frame.
presentation.slideTwoTitle: The margin is driven by the viewport, not by content
presentation.slideTwoBody: The outer container keeps a safe offset from the screen edges, while slide width is constrained by both viewport height and width. This keeps the layout stable on 16:10 and narrower screens.
presentation.slideThreeTitle: Scrolling behaves like a vertical deck
presentation.slideThreeBody: Each slide occupies a full 100dvh step and uses scroll snap, so page scrolling moves the viewer slide by slide. This gives presentation-like behavior without a separate navigation engine.

notes.title: Speaker notes for the BRIEF presentation deck
notes.text1: The working notes for this deck are maintained in the project documentation under <path>docs/notes.md</path>.

slide1.title: A <focus>Rights-Based Governance Framework</focus> for AI-Enabled Human Enhancement
slide1.subtitle: Introducing the <codex>Codex of Augmented Humanity</codex> as a governance layer for structural risks in AI-enabled augmentation

slide2.title: Continuous socio-technical augmentation outpaces sectoral regulation

cards.title: The Shift
cards.text1: Enhancement is evolving from episodic clinical interventions into continuous, AI-driven systems.

cards.title: The Gap
cards.text1: EU laws such as GDPR, the AI Act, and medical-device rules operate in isolated silos, failing to address continuous, connected augmentation.

cards.title: The Resolution
cards.text1: The Codex of Augmented Humanity translates fundamental EU rights such as dignity and bodily integrity into operational design constraints that prevent systemic exploitation.

slide3.title: AI alters the fundamental architecture of biological intervention

rows.right: Adaptive machine learning and ongoing optimization

slide4.title: The continuous feedback loop of AI-enabled augmentation

cards.title: Sensing Layer
cards.text1: Collects continuous telemetry, physiological, and behavioral data directly from the body.

cards.title: AI Layer
cards.text1: Performs predictive classification and adaptive optimization, often with external vendor infrastructure in the loop.

cards.title: Actuation Layer
cards.text1: Dynamically modifies physical or cognitive treatment parameters in real time.

cards.title: Oversight Layer
cards.text1: Manages logging, human validation limits, and regulatory compliance.

slide5.title: Sectoral EU frameworks fail to capture systemic infrastructural risks

panels.text1: Existing regulations are robust but isolated. None independently address the convergence of functional body integration, algorithmic adaptability, and economic embeddedness.
Long-term dependency, continuous optimization, and contractual lock-in.
Enhancement must never become a channel for exploitation, particularly where life-sustaining functions or core autonomy are at stake.

slide6.title: Threat analysis must now assume adversarial environments

panels.text1: shifts risk away from isolated clinical defects toward structural coercion.
Algorithms rapidly scale interventions and turn intimate health data into predictive tools.
must account for hierarchies, market leverage, and competitive pressures, not just benign therapeutic use.

slide7.title: Militarization generates structural pressure to upgrade

panels.text1: Upgrading resilience or perception creates upgrade pressure inside hierarchies, eroding meaningful consent. Therapeutic capabilities can quickly pivot into strategic defense assets.
Strictly prohibit coercive enhancement as a condition of service or employment. Impose mandatory long-term medical support obligations for irreversible interventions.

slide8.title: Connectivity transforms implants into cyber-physical attack surfaces

panels.text1: Remote configuration concentrates practical control in the hands of vendors or malicious actors. A compromised wireless insulin pump can directly alter biological states and produce severe harm.
Life-critical implants must not be remotely controllable by default. Secure offline degraded modes must preserve life when networks fail.

slide9.title: AI optimization lowers the barrier for biologically targeted harm

panels.text1: AI accelerates the design, simulation, and parameter optimization of biological interventions. Misuse now requires little more than data access and compute.
Impose an absolute ban on enhancement-related biological research intended to harm or persecute. Require strict dual-use reviews for AI-accelerated biological scaling.

slide10.title: Continuous telemetry enables hidden social sorting
slide10.mechanismText: AI transforms intimate biological markers into scalable risk-scoring systems. Access to basic opportunities becomes probabilistically conditioned on continuous biological metrics.
constraints: Absolute ban on enhancement-based discrimination by insurers, employers, or platforms.
Strict purpose limitation to prevent general-surveillance function creep.

slide11.title: Proprietary infrastructure creates pay-to-live economic coercion

panels.title: The Mechanism
panels.text1: When essential bodily functions rely on proprietary software or continuous vendor support, market power translates directly into leverage over bodily integrity.

panels.title: Codex Normative Constraints
panels.text1: Prohibit design patterns that throttle or disable life-sustaining functions for financial leverage. Mandate credible exit and software-escrow mechanisms for durable dependency relationships.

slide12.title: Optional optimization normalizes into structural requirements
slide12.mechanismText: In highly competitive environments such as sport, enhancement shifts physiological baselines. What begins as an optional upgrade can become a structural prerequisite for participation.

constraints.title: Ensure integrity rules do not inadvertently discriminate
constraints.text1: against individuals using legitimate therapeutic interventions.

slide13.title: The Codex translates fundamental rights into operational constraints

rows.principle: Prohibit coercive enhancement and ensure meaningful consent
Ban harmful applications and enforce dual-use review
Restrict remote control and mandate fail-safe offline design
Enforce purpose limitation and prohibit algorithmic discrimination
Guarantee continuity and prohibit exploitative functional control

slide14.title: Governance must match the continuous structure of the technology

sections.title: Design-Time Constraints:
sections.text1: Hard-coded physical and software limits, such as disabling remote overrides of critical biological functions.

sections.title: Lifecycle Obligations:
sections.text1: Guaranteed long-term security updates, continuous auditability, and mandatory offline degraded modes.

sections.title: Institutional Enforcement:
sections.text1: Mandatory certification, compliance audits, and proactive oversight spanning both medical and digital domains.

slide15.title: A structural precondition for human dignity
statements: AI-assisted augmentation possesses immense potential to restore health, independence, and capacity.
A professional Codex is not an optional ethical supplement. It is an operational necessity.
By embedding EU fundamental rights directly into system architecture, augmentation can serve the human being instead of instrumentalizing them.

poster.title: Scientific poster
poster.lead: This section is intended to present a conference poster in a clear static web format.
poster.description: You can place the research summary, key findings, authors, affiliations, and supporting references here. The layout remains lightweight and compatible with static deployment.

backgroundCards.title: Defining AIgmented HUMANity
backgroundCards.text1: A near-term integration of AI with mechanical and biological enhancement focused on preventive care, personalized intervention, and longevity support.

backgroundCards.title: The Evolutionary Shift
backgroundCards.text1: A near-term integration of AI with mechanical and biological enhancement focused on preventive care, personalized intervention, and longevity support.

backgroundCards.title: Timeline of Human Enhancement
backgroundCards.text1: Evolution from early spectacles and prosthetics to modern gene editing and AI-enabled decision support.

legalFramework.title: Regulatory baseline for the poster argument

cards.title: GDPR and Special Data Categories
cards.text1: Genetic, biometric, and health data are classified as ‘special categories’ with a general prohibition on processing unless narrow exceptions apply.

cards.title: The EU AI Act
cards.text1: Classifies most AI components in medical devices as ‘high-risk’, requiring binding obligations for documentation and oversight.

cards.title: EHDS and Human Rights
cards.text1: The European Health Data Space (EHDS) and the Oviedo Convention protect against genetic discrimination and ensure secure data circulation.

centralDiagram.title: Key labels and concentric governance layers
centralDiagram.labelsCardTitle: Side labels around the central illustration

rings.title: Data Governance
rings.text1: Handles sensitive genomic and health data. Access control and purpose limitation.

rings.title: AI Decision-Making
rings.text1: Focuses on risk prediction and personalization models. Uses subgroup validation to prevent bias.

rings.title: Oversight & Compliance
rings.text1: Ensures auditability, human-in-the-loop control, and post-market monitoring.

ethicalIssues.title: Main risk domains discussed on the poster

cards.title: Militarization and Dual-Use
cards.text1: Performance augmentation in conflict, neural interfaces blurring human agency, and the “dual-use” risk of gene-editing technologies.

cards.title: Data Misuse by Insurers and Employers
cards.text1: Risk: “genetic redlining” where biometric or genetic data is used as a covert tool for underwriting or employment screening.

cards.title: Algorithmic Bias in Health AI
cards.text1: Systems may amplify structural inequities, such as pulse oximeters performing poorly on darker skin or algorithms using healthcare spending as a proxy for medical need.

cards.title: Irreversibility of Genetic Data
cards.text1: Unlike passwords, genomic data cannot be changed; breaches pose permanent privacy and equity risks.

cards.title: Structural Re-Identification Risk
cards.text1: ‘Anonymized’ genomic datasets can often be re-identified using public reference databases and surname inference.

cards.title: Five Core Patient Rights
cards.text1: Under GDPR, patients hold the rights to Access, Rectification, Erasure, Data Portability, and meaningful Human Review of automated decisions.

riskMatrix.title: Readable mobile version of the matrix

rows.risk: Misuse of genetic and health data
rows.control: Data minimization, access control, purpose limitation (GDPR)
Subgroup validation, bias monitoring, representative training data

conclusion.title: Poster takeaway
paragraphs: The convergence of artificial intelligence with biological and mechanical forms of human enhancement represents a near-term and technically feasible paradigm shift in healthcare and human performance support.

A structured compliance-by-design framework translates ethical and legal requirements into system-level constraints, including data governance constraints, bias-aware model validation, human-in-the-loop decision processes, and auditability of system behavior.

This approach reframes AI-driven human enhancement as a system design problem, where governance requirements must be embedded directly into AI architectures to ensure deployment without enabling discrimination, exploitation, or state-level misuse.
